input_candidates.truth_value: Cracks propagating in δ-ferrite lamellae are arrested and blunted at carbide/ferrite interfaces; carbides increase effective crack tip curvature radius, reducing stress concentration.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Carbides pin dislocations and increase work hardening rate of composite lamellae; part of interfacial stress between ferrite matrix and carbides is transferred to carbides, alleviating stress concentration.

input_candidates.truth_value: Stress concentration at crack tip in δ-ferrite lamellae is relieved by interface separation in carbide/ferrite lamellae.
input_candidates.prediction_value: During impact, interface separation occurs at lamellar boundaries dispersing crack-tip stress on the separation surface; spherical carbides increase effective curvature radius of crack tip reducing stress concentration factor according to elasticity theory.

input_candidates.truth_value: Coarser carbides provide less blunting effect, leading to reduced crack tip curvature and easier crack propagation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Weakened Orowan effect due to carbide coarsening and reduced distribution density; coarse particles have weakened ability to disperse stress at crack tip.

input_candidates.truth_value: Weakened dispersion of stress by coarser carbides.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Coarse carbides at grain boundaries and fine carbides inside grains due to Ostwald ripening; reduced crack deflection resistance and lower number of microvoid nucleation sites compared to R650.

input_candidates.truth_value: Large hardness mismatch causes stress concentration at δ-ferrite/martensite and martensite/DIF interfaces; equiaxed DIF grains act as pre-existing defects promoting brittle fracture.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.0 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.6 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and preferential crack initiation sites.

input_candidates.truth_value: Interfacial stress concentration leads to crack initiation at boundaries.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Large hardness mismatch between martensite, DIF, and δ-ferrite induces stress concentration at phase interfaces; equiaxed DIF grains act as pre-existing defects guiding rapid cleavage crack propagation.

input_candidates.truth_value: At high temperature, carbides dissolve completely; DIFT (deformation-induced ferrite transformation) occurs during rolling, forming equiaxed ferrite grains within austenite, which later transforms to martensite, resulting in honeycomb structure.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Carbides completely dissolved at high temperature; austenite stabilized by Mn and high temperature; after rolling, residual austenite transformed to martensite, resulting in honeycomb martensite lamellae with embedded equiaxed DIF grains.

input_candidates.truth_value: Similar to R850 but with larger martensite fraction and coarser DIF grains, further reducing toughness.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Maximum hardness difference reaches 3.86 GPa (martensite 6.5 GPa vs δ-ferrite 2.8 GPa), causing stress concentration at phase boundaries and brittle fracture behavior.

input_candidates.truth_value: Stress concentration at interfaces promotes brittle crack propagation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Martensite/ferrite hardness mismatch >3.3 GPa hinders deformation coordination; stress concentration at phase boundaries combined with DIF grains acting as pre-existing defects leads to unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.

input_candidates.truth_value: With increasing temperature, DIFT and DRX produce more equiaxed ferrite grains and coarser martensite lamellae; all carbides dissolved.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Increased martensite fraction (59.0%) and DIF grain coarsening compared to R850; high-temperature rolling significantly degrades toughness through microstructure coarsening and brittle phase formation.

input_candidates.truth_value: SPP (strength-plasticity product) for R650: 16.5 ± 1.5 GPa%, R750: 12.5 ± 2.6 GPa%, R850: 9.6 ± 0.1 GPa%, R950: 4.4 ± 1.0 GPa%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: As-forged control microstructure (before rolling) showed no obvious lamellar structure, consisting mainly of coarse δ-ferrite and M/A constituents (Fig. 3a-c).

input_candidates.truth_value: R650 impact crack initiation energy Ei = 59.6 J, propagation energy Ep = 147.9 J; R750 Ep = 51.51 J, indicating dominant role of propagation resistance.
input_candidates.prediction_value: R650 Charpy impact test detailed energy partition: crack initiation energy Ei = 59.6 J, propagation energy Ep = 147.9 J; R750 Ep = 51.51 J. High-temperature rolled specimens (R850, R950) exhibited steep single-peak impact load-displacement curves with immediate unstable crack propagation after initiation.

input_candidates.truth_value: R850 and R950 impact curves show steep single peak with almost no Ei or Ep, indicating unstable crack propagation immediately after initiation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Strength-plasticity product (SPP) values: R650 16.51 GPa%, R750 12.5 GPa%, R850 9.6 GPa%, R950 4.4 GPa%.

input_candidates.truth_value: Low-temperature rolling zone (R650, R750) exhibits DRV (dynamic recovery) as dominant softening mechanism with high KAM values; high-temperature rolling zone (R850, R950) dominated by DRX (dynamic recrystallization) and DIFT, leading to HAGBs increase and dislocation density decrease.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Work hardening rate (θ) curves (Fig. 5e-f) for low-temperature rolled specimens showed a typical hump shape (rapid decrease at ε<0.02, slight rise, slow decay at ε≥0.03) due to Orowan pinning, whereas high-temperature rolled specimens showed monotonic decrease indicating lack of coordinated deformation capacity; exact numerical coordinates not provided in text.

input_candidates.truth_value: Carbide precipitation mechanism: during low-temperature rolling, martensite decomposes, C, Mn, Cr diffuse to defect sites and precipitate as M3C, M23C6, M7C7; Ostwald ripening at higher temperature coarsens carbides.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Ostwald ripening mechanism caused carbide coarsening in R750: small carbides dissolve and diffuse via grain boundaries and dislocations to precipitate on larger carbides, producing coarse carbides at grain boundaries plus fine carbides inside grains.

input_candidates.truth_value: R650 fracture mode: mixed ductile fracture with crack blunting, deflection, and microvoid-induced horizontal propagation; tensile fracture shows delamination serrated morphology.
input_candidates.prediction_value: In the high-temperature rolling zone, DIFT produced equiaxed ferrite grains inside honeycomb martensite lamellae; DIF grains had higher C and Mn but lower Al than original δ-ferrite, contributing to higher hardness via solid solution and Hall-Petch strengthening.

input_candidates.truth_value: Orowan mechanism: carbides with diameter >10 nm pin dislocations, enhancing work hardening; nanoscale spherical carbides increase crack tip curvature radius and blunt crack, reducing stress concentration.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dislocation density was calculated using the formula ρ = 2θ/(μb) where θ is average KAM, μ is step size, and b is Burgers vector.

input_candidates.truth_value: Bonding force at carbide/ferrite interface is greater than at ferrite/ferrite interface, preventing crack propagation through carbide-containing lamellae.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Phase evolution with temperature: at 650–750°C carbides are stable and form C/F composite lamellae; at 850–950°C carbides fully dissolve into austenite, resulting in δ-ferrite plus honeycomb martensite lamellae after cooling.

input_candidates.truth_value: In high-temperature rolling zone, hardness mismatch martensite/δ-ferrite >3.3 GPa causes interfacial stress concentration; DIF grains act as pre-fabricated crack paths.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Low-temperature rolled specimens exhibited excellent deformation coordination between hard C/F composite lamellae and soft δ-ferrite lamellae, producing serrated tensile fracture morphology with delamination characteristics (Fig. 8c).

input_candidates.truth_value: Figure 4: EBSD maps (GB+GOS, KAM, IPF, nanoindentation) for all samples; Figure 5: mechanical properties including impact energy vs temperature, load-displacement curves, engineering stress-strain curves, work hardening rate curves; Figure 6: phase diagrams; Figure 7: TEM/EDS of R650 carbides; Figure 8: impact and tensile fracture morphology of R650; Figure 9: TEM/HRTEM of R650 and fracture schematics for all samples. Pure text pipeline does not include image pixel data.
